Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a fungal disease causing olive-green to brown spots on leaves and fruit, often leading to deformed or cracked fruit.
treatment: Apply fungicide (myclobutanil or captan) at bud break and every 7-10 days during wet weather
Rake and destroy fallen leaves to reduce fungal spores
Prune trees to improve air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease causing circular lesions on leaves and rotting of fruit, starting as small purple spots.
treatment: Prune and destroy all infected branches, mummified fruit, and dead wood
Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicide
Remove fallen fruit and leaves from the ground
Ensure good air circulation through regular pruning

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is a fungal disease causing bright orange-yellow spots on leaves and fruit, requiring both apple and cedar trees to complete its lifecycle.
treatment: Apply myclobutanil or triadimefon fungicide from pink bud stage through early summer
Remove nearby cedar or juniper trees if possible
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties for future plantings
Rake and destroy fallen leaves
Continue regular watering and fertilizing
Monitor leaves regularly for early signs of disease
Prune annually to maintain good air circulation
Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5
Water regularly, keeping soil moist but not waterlogged
Apply acidic fertilizer in spring

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew is a fungal disease causing a white powdery coating on leaves, shoots, and sometimes fruit.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based or potassium bicarbonate fungicide
Remove and destroy heavily infected shoots
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes susceptible growth
Improve air circulation through pruning
Water at the base to keep foliage dry
Fertilize in early spring
Monitor for pests and disease regularly

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is a fungal disease causing rectangular gray to tan lesions on corn leaves, which can significantly reduce yield.
treatment: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicide at early tasseling stage
Rotate crops — avoid planting corn in the same field consecutively
Till soil to bury infected crop residue
Plant resistant hybrids in future seasons

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust causes small, circular to elongated cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces.
treatment: Apply triazole or strobilurin fungicide if infection is severe
Plant rust-resistant corn hybrids
Monitor fields regularly during humid conditions
Early planting can help avoid peak rust season

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight causes long, cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions on corn leaves.
treatment: Apply fungicide (propiconazole or azoxystrobin) at early disease onset
Plant resistant hybrids
Rotate crops and till residue to reduce spore load
Avoid fields with history of northern leaf blight
Ensure adequate nitrogen fertilization
Monitor for pests such as corn borers
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease causing brown lesions on leaves and black, shriveled mummified fruit.
treatment: Apply myclobutanil or mancozeb fungicide from bud break through veraison
Remove and destroy all mummified fruit and infected leaves
Prune to improve canopy air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca is a complex fungal disease causing tiger-stripe patterns on leaves, berry spotting, and eventual vine decline.
treatment: Prune out and destroy infected wood during dry weather
Protect pruning wounds with fungicide or wound sealant
There is no cure — focus on prevention and removing infected vines
Avoid water stress which makes vines more susceptible

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Leaf blight causes irregular dark brown spots on grape leaves, often leading to premature defoliation.
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide or mancozeb
Remove and destroy infected leaves
Improve air circulation through canopy management
Avoid wetting foliage when irrigating
Prune annually to maintain open canopy
Monitor for fungal diseases during wet weather
Fertilize appropriately based on soil tests

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Citrus greening (HLB) is a devastating bacterial disease spread by the Asian citrus psyllid, causing yellowing, misshapen bitter fruit, and eventual tree death. There is currently no cure.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).urgency: Act immediately — report to local agricultural authorities
treatment: Remove and destroy infected trees to prevent spread
Control Asian citrus psyllid with insecticides
Report to your local agricultural extension office — HLB is a notifiable disease
Do not move plant material between regions
Plant certified disease-free nursery stock only

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot causes water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown and fall out, giving a shot-hole appearance, and causes pitting on fruit.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide during early leaf development
Avoid overhead irrigation
Prune to improve air circulation
Plant resistant peach varieties for future plantings
Thin fruit to improve size and reduce disease pressure
Water at the base to keep foliage dry
Apply dormant oil spray in late winter

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot causes water-soaked lesions on leaves and fruit that turn brown and scabby, reducing marketability.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide every 5-7 days during wet weather
Use certified disease-free seed
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet
Rotate crops — avoid planting peppers in the same location for 2-3 years

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: Your bell pepper plant appears healthy!
treatment: Water consistently to prevent blossom end rot
Fertilize with balanced fertilizer
Monitor for aphids and other pests

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight causes dark brown spots with concentric rings on older leaves, leading to yellowing and defoliation.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide every 7-10 days
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Ensure adequate potassium fertilization to improve resistance
Avoid overhead irrigation and water in the morning

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is a highly destructive disease caused by Phytophthora infestans, causing water-soaked lesions that rapidly turn brown and can destroy an entire crop within days in wet conditions.
Potato___Late_blight.urgency: Treat immediately — can spread extremely rapidly
treatment: Apply fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or cymoxanil) immediately
Remove and destroy all infected plant material — do not compost
Hill soil around plants to protect tubers
Harvest tubers as soon as possible if infection is severe
Do not plant saved tubers from infected crops
Hill soil around plants as they grow
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle
Water consistently and avoid waterlogging
Prune out old canes after fruiting
Maintain good air circulation
Water at the base to keep foliage dry
Monitor for soybean aphids and other pests
Ensure adequate soil moisture during pod fill
Rotate with non-legume crops

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew causes white powdery patches on leaves and stems, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor.
treatment: Apply potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, or sulfur fungicide
Remove heavily infected leaves
Avoid overhead watering
Plant resistant varieties in future seasons

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch causes small purple spots on leaves that enlarge and cause leaf margins to appear scorched and brown.
treatment: Apply myclobutanil or captan fungicide
Remove and destroy infected leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation
Renovate beds after harvest by mowing and thinning plants
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Remove runners to maintain plant vigor
Fertilize after harvest

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot causes small water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit that turn brown, reducing yield and fruit quality.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide every 5-7 days
Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Avoid working with plants when wet
Rotate crops for 2-3 years

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight causes dark brown spots with concentric rings on older leaves, leading to yellowing and defoliation from the bottom up.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide every 7-10 days
Remove infected lower leaves
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Water at the base in the morning

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is a devastating disease causing large water-soaked lesions on leaves and brown rot on fruit, capable of destroying a crop within days.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicide immediately
Remove and destroy all infected plant material
Avoid overhead irrigation
If infection is severe, consider destroying the entire plant to protect others

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mold causes pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green to brown mold on the undersides.
treatment: Improve greenhouse ventilation to reduce humidity
Apply copper-based or chlorothalonil fungicide
Remove and destroy infected leaves
Avoid overhead watering

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot causes circular spots with dark borders and light centers on lower leaves, spreading upward.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicide every 7-10 days
Remove infected leaves immediately
Mulch to prevent soil splash
Rotate crops for 2-3 years

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Spider mites cause stippled, yellowing leaves with fine webbing on the undersides, and thrive in hot dry conditions.
treatment: Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil, covering leaf undersides thoroughly
Spray plants with water to dislodge mites
Introduce predatory mites as biological control
Avoid water stress which makes plants more susceptible

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot causes circular brown lesions with concentric rings resembling a target on leaves, stems, and fruit.
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin fungicide
Remove and destroy infected plant material
Improve air circulation through pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus is spread by whiteflies, causing upward curling and yellowing of leaves, stunted growth, and severe yield loss. There is no cure.
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread
Control whitefly populations with insecticides or sticky traps
Use reflective mulch to deter whiteflies
Plant virus-resistant tomato varieties
Install insect-proof netting in greenhouses

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus causes mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves, distortion, and reduced fruit quality. There is no cure.
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants
Wash hands and disinfect tools after handling infected plants
Do not smoke near plants — tobacco can carry the virus
Plant resistant varieties
Control aphids which can spread the virus
Water consistently at the base to prevent blossom end rot
Stake or cage plants for support
Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pests
